titleStmt.title: Memorandum of the Substance of Discussion at a Department of State–Joint Chiefs of Staff Meeting

head.hi: Memorandum of the Substance of Discussion at a Department of State–Joint Chiefs of Staff Meeting

note.p: A note on the title page read: “State Draft. Not cleared with any of participants.”

Of the JCS, Generals Bradley and Collins as well as Vice Chiefs General Twining and Admiral Duncan attended. Bowie headed the Department of State contingent; General Ruffner and Admiral Page Smith represented the Department of Defense. General Cabell attended for the CIA and Gleason for the NSC. In all, 24 persons were present. The entire meeting was devoted to Korea.

div.p:
Mr. Johnson: We have been working with your people over the week end on the draft message to Clark and have had discussions with General Smith on the general question of our present posture in the Korean negotiations. We have the feeling that we just can’t stand on our present position that the Korean POWs should be released to civilian status upon the conclusion of an armistice. The Commies have been exploiting this as the weakest point in our position, one which our Allies give us no support on and one which is really so illogical that it tends to weaken our whole position. We fully recognize the problems that Clark and Briggs will have with Rhee on shifting from this position, but we believe the problem that we have to meet is that of how we handle Rhee when we change our position. We also feel that a provision for referring the question of the non-repatriates to the U.N. would make our position closer to that of the Indian Resolution and would assist in retrieving Allied support. On the question of unanimity in the voting of the custodial commission, we realize that the President faces a real domestic political problem if that position should be changed. The position was originally adopted by our Secretary after consultations with members of the Congress.A record of these consultations can be found in a memorandum for the files by Morton, dated May 11, 1953, on a meeting with Congressional leaders, May 9, 1953, not printed. (611.95A241/5–1153) A second report of this meeting can be found in a memorandum by Dulles, May 9, 1953, not printed. (795.00/5–953) A shift to decision by a
majority might, therefore, raise problems for the President with the Congress.
General Collins: I don’t see how we can really stick to the unanimity principle. I don’t see how the commission could do anything if it operated under it.

Mr. Robertson: It really is a question of form, not substance, if the terms of reference are clear. There already is in our position a provision for majority vote on questions of procedure. We don’t really get anything by sticking on unanimity. What is important is getting clear terms of reference.
Mr. Johnson: We also have the feeling that in the presentation of the final position we should, to the degree possible, avoid appearing to be handing out an ultimatum.
General Eddleman: I would like to raise the question about your provision for executive sessions.

Mr. Johnson: We feel strongly that our negotiators should go into executive sessions. In setting forth our final position we can’t completely avoid giving it some of the aspects of an ultimatum, but putting it out in public session would make it even more difficult to avoid the ultimatum aspect of our proposal. We also think that in our presentation we should attempt to bring it out that we are accepting a large part of the Communists’ position. We should present our position, we believe, in the form of general acceptance of their position rather than in the form of a counter proposal. General Smith, when we discussed this draft telegram with him, felt that it wasn’t clear enough for Clark and that it was too complicated to use in informing the President of the decisions that he had to make, so we should try and get something up by this afternoon from which General Smith can talk to the President. We have also been discussing the question of how best we can make use of Bohlen in Moscow. There has been the tentative suggestion that we might have Chip present our position to the Russians and then leave it up to the Commies as to whether they propose it and have us accept, or vice versa. There is the alternative suggestion that we might present it to the Russians through Chip simultaneously with our presentation of our position in the negotiations. We have also been discussing the desirability of getting our Allies in and getting them on the line to support our position publicly.

Mr. Robertson: What the Communists are doing is capitalizing on the difference between us and our Allies. If our Allies would only stand firm we might have less of a problem bringing the Communists around to our position, but our Allies won’t. I would also like to point out that I believe it is important that we stick by the detailed agreements of the terms of reference which protect the prisoners who don’t want to go back from pressure or beating up. We have a lot of North Koreans, to speak only of them, who in spite of the fact that they have not been
given any indication of what was going to happen to them eventually are still unwilling to go back to North Korea. If we should put them in a position now where they were beaten up or pressured to go back to North Korea, it would undermine our whole political position against the Communists.

Mr. Johnson: I agree that it is important for us to hold to the detailed terms of reference, but I think we should realize that we will be under very heavy pressure if the Communists agree to general principles and we go on doing what our Allies will undoubtedly describe as “haggling over details”.
General Bradley: Already our own papers are leaning towards the position of blaming the JCS for blocking an armistice by haggling over details.

Mr. Robertson: I think the difficulty is that they evidently don’t read Harrison’s statements carefully and they don’t use those statements. Actually it seems to me that Harrison has done a very good job if the newspapers would only make fuller use of what he says.
Mr. Johnson: It seems to me that we should discuss our substantive position on the question of the Korean prisoners, on unanimity, and on reference of the POWs to the U.N.
General Bradley: I don’t think we will get an armistice if we don’t change our position on the Korean POWs.
General Collins: It seems to me that essentially that is a political decision and not a military question.
Admiral Duncan: The real question is what Rhee will agree to and I should think the first thing to do is find out what he is willing to agree on and what he wants.
Mr. Johnson: We know all too well already what Rhee wants. He wants to get to the Yalu with our help and if he can’t get that he wants a security pact.
General Collins: Is the State position firmed up on what recommendations you would make to the President on a security pact?

Mr. Robertson: This would be a decision the President himself would have to make. We think it would be useful with Rhee to propose a security pact but our own Secretary has been against it on the grounds that it would detract from the U.S. aspects of the Korean effort and that we could put ourselves in a position of guaranteeing Rhee’s control over North Korea when he doesn’t control it.

General Collins: The question of the security pact does have military aspects. If we give the Koreans a guarantee and if we were going to implement that guarantee, we would have to have military forces near enough to support the guarantee. They would have to be either in Korea or in Japan. The Japanese will eventually want to have us out of Japan and I don’t know how we can then support a guarantee to Korea. If we would leave forces in Korea, we would be leaving forces
in a place where in the event of global war we might not want to fight, so from a military point of view there are real disadvantages to a security pact with the Koreans.
Mr. Robertson: The question of what we can give Rhee to make him go along with our position in the armistice negotiation is precisely what should be put to the President.

Mr. Johnson: Briggs’ reaction to the draft statement which we asked for his comments on was that it didn’t really serve the purpose.See unnumbered telegram from Seoul, supra , May 17, 1953, which commented on the proposals contained in telegram 713 to Pusan, May 15, p. 1029.

General Bradley: I have some worries about the question of referring the POW matter to the U.N. We were unhappy about that before. Could we accept it now? Of course I realize that we probably really couldn’t break on something that we had previously accepted.

Mr. Johnson: The President has publicly supported the U.N. Resolution and Ambassador Lodge has characterized it as a standard to which all honest men could repair. Under these circumstances we couldn’t possibly object to it if the Commies should propose it and from the point of view of our relations with our Allies we probably couldn’t break off the negotiations without having made it clear that we were prepared to accept it. It is, of course, possible that the Communists themselves might not want to refer the thing to the U.N.

General Eddleman: What Johnson is proposing is really a complete re-do of the message to Clark. I would like to discuss an interim message to Clark which would give him authority to call for a further recess. (At this point General Eddleman circulated a draft telegram.This draft was transmitted to Clark in final form as DA 939211, May 18, 1953. In it Clark was informed that “action designed to bring matters to a head” at Panmunjom would require prior approval “at highest level of US Government as well as close coordination with our allies.” These actions could not be accomplished in time to allow presentation of new proposals to the Communists on May 20. Consequently, Clark was asked to extend the current recess 5 days. (Matthews files, lot 53 D 413))

Admiral Duncan: It seems to me that the part of the message which says that we think he should talk to Rhee before making the new proposal needs clarification. What is important is the terms in which he is going to talk to Rhee.
Mr. Robertson: One of the questions General Smith is going to see the President about this afternoon is just how far we can go in talking with Rhee.

General Collins: If the President is going to talk to the Congress and State to the Ambassadors, and Clark and Briggs are going to talk to Rhee and then we are going to have to act on what Rhee proposes, I think that instead of a two or three day recess we will need at least a week.

General Bradley: We can’t leave the question of the position up to Rhee. We will have to tell him what our final position is and say what
we can or cannot do about a security pact. Then if he won’t go along, we will have to take action against him.
General Collins: But Rhee is bound to come back with some sort of reaction and we will have to consult on that here in Washington.

Colonel Matthews: If you consult with Rhee on our final position, then we will actually be carrying on a firming up of our final position in the public press. If we don’t want to discuss our final position in the newspapers, we will have to tell Rhee about it not more than two or three hours before the meeting and then go ahead and act on our final position.
General Bradley: I think you have a point there.

General Eddleman: You might have a clear idea as to how much time we will need after we talk with the President this afternoon. Why don’t we draft up the message but leave the number of days blank. Then Johnson can let us know later after the session with the President.

General Bradley: I think what we need to do is get up a memorandum for the PresidentFor a copy of this memorandum in its final form, see p. 1046. which would include the points that we think we can’t stand on our position on the Korean POWs, that we can’t stand on the unanimity principle, and that although we would prefer to turn the POWs loose we think that probably we will have to make it clear that we will accept reference to the U.N.
Mr. Johnson: Having already accepted the idea once, we can’t stand against referral to the U.N. if the Communists should offer it.
Mr. Robertson: But the Communists have not been asking for U.N. consideration and apparently none of our Allies have objected to the release of the POWs after a fixed time limit.
Mr. Bowie: It is my understanding that the Indians have pointed out that our present position differs from the General Assembly resolution in not allowing for referral to the U.N.
Mr. Johnson: Our Allies are not pressing us hard on it right now but I would agree we can’t stand against it if the Communists should bring it up.
Admiral Duncan: This is a point I can’t agree on. We were unhappy about the Indian Resolution before and I don’t see why we have to accept it now.

General Bradley: I think we do. We have committed ourselves to it before and we would be in an inconsistent position if we now said we were opposed to it. I had another question. Do we stand on the position of no Commie forces?
Mr. Robertson: Yes, we should. It will be perfectly understandable to our Allies that we are unwilling to let Communist armed forces come into our rear or have responsibility for POWs.

General Bradley: On the point of what we do if the Communists reject our final position, I think it raises two questions, both of which are primarily political. What will be the effect on the Allies of breaking and what will be the effect on the Commies? When will you discuss this with our Allies? Shouldn’t we get our Allies to go along with us now on a decision to break off negotiations if the Communists don’t accept our final proposal?
General Eddleman: If the Commies give a flat rejection, can’t Harrison break off immediately?

Mr. Matthews: I don’t think the Communists will be so helpful as to give us a clear, flat rejection, but in any case the decision for breaking off the talks should not be left to Harrison. It must be made here in Washington.
General Bradley: Are you going to ask our Allies now as to whether or not they will support us in breaking off the negotiations?

Mr. Matthews: I would be inclined to tell our Allies now that this is our final position and get their agreement that this is a reasonable final position, then at the time when the Communists don’t meet the position, we could tell our Allies that we have made it clear to them that this was a final position, that there was no use in talking any more since the Communists have rejected it and that we were terminating the talks.
General Bradley: I have a real question on the section of the talks about repudiating all former agreements and withdrawing all U.N. proposals.
Admiral Fechteler: I think we would look tougher if we would break off clean and make it clear that all bets were off, that any new negotiations would start from scratch.

General Collins: I don’t see any use in withdrawing everything, including the part of the armistice that has already been agreed upon. If we want to repudiate the agreements that have been made up to now, we could do that at any time that the situation changes. Unless we are really ready to go all out in Korea, it seems to me we might well want to preserve the tentative agreements that have been made so far, but it seems to me this is largely a political question.
Mr. Bowie: We will have to get the President’s approval on a message authorizing a final break anyway. Perhaps it will be best to postpone a decision on that until that time.
Admiral Fechteler: I didn’t quite understand what the question was of using Chip Bohlen in these negotiations.

Mr. Johnson: It was our thought that we would present our proposals through Chip to the Russians and have him tell them how seriously we took them simultaneously with Harrison’s giving them to the Communists in Panmunjom.

Admiral Fechteler: I thought there was some question of presenting them first in Moscow and waiting for the Communists’ reaction. I think
the idea of putting them forward simultaneously is a good one but I have real question about leaving it up to the Russians as to how a proposal should be handled.
Mr. Johnson: That was a tentative suggestion but by and large I think we are thinking primarily of transmitting them simultaneously.
